title: Virtual Threads (Java 21)
objectives: Understand virtual threads through a real-life analogy
See how virtual threads let Java scale to millions of concurrent I/O tasks
Distinguish platform threads from virtual threads
Understand pinning — the one subtle gotcha to avoid

sections.markdown:
**Imagine an airport with a limited number of runways (say 8).**

- **Old way (platform threads):** every plane parks on a runway. If you have more planes than runways, they queue at the terminal — no more can land. You can have at most 8 planes at a time.
- **New way (virtual threads):** planes park at **cheap gates** (millions available). When a plane needs a runway (I/O), it briefly uses one — then vacates. Runways serve thousands of planes per hour because most planes spend most of their time *at the gate*, not on the runway.

**A virtual thread is a plane at a gate.** The runways (OS threads = 'carrier threads') are limited. When the virtual thread blocks on I/O, it *unmounts* from its carrier — freeing the runway for another virtual thread. When the I/O completes, it *remounts* on any carrier and continues.

**Result:** simple, blocking, easy-to-read code that scales to millions of concurrent tasks. Java 21 finally has what Go had a decade ago.

**Virtual threads (Project Loom, finalized in Java 21)** are lightweight threads managed by the JVM — not the OS. Millions can run concurrently. When a virtual thread blocks on I/O, it unmounts from its carrier OS thread, freeing the carrier for other work. The result: simple thread-per-request code that scales like async code.
## Platform vs virtual threads — side by side

rows: Wastes an OS thread while blocked

sections.markdown: **The revelation of virtual threads:** you can go back to writing simple, blocking, thread-per-request code — and it scales. Before Loom, servers needed reactive frameworks (WebFlux, RxJava) with callback chains to handle 10,000 connections. Now: `while (true) { handleRequest(socket.accept()); }` inside a virtual thread executor works for a million connections. **Simplicity + performance.**
## Creating and using virtual threads

## Pinning — the one gotcha

A virtual thread normally *unmounts* from its carrier when it blocks — that's the whole magic. But two situations can **pin** it (prevent unmounting), holding the carrier hostage:

1. **Blocking inside a `synchronized` block or method.**
2. **Calling native code (JNI) that blocks.**

Pinned virtual threads hold their carrier for the whole blocking duration — defeating the point. Under heavy load with lots of pinning, you can starve the carrier pool.

**Prefer `ReentrantLock` over `synchronized` in hot I/O paths** with virtual threads. `ReentrantLock` doesn't cause pinning. Java 24 aims to eliminate synchronized-caused pinning entirely — but until then, be aware.
## When virtual threads help — and when they don't

rows: Thousands of HTTP requests, mostly I/O
Each request naps waiting on network → carriers freed
The work needs a CPU; virtualizing doesn't help

sections.markdown: ## A complete sample program — 1 million virtual threads sleeping in parallel
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Using virtual threads for CPU-bound work.**

Virtual threads don't add CPU cores. If you have 8 cores and 1000 virtual threads doing pure math, only 8 run at once. Use a fixed platform-thread pool sized to `Runtime.availableProcessors()` for CPU work. Virtual threads are for I/O.

**Mistake 2: Pooling virtual threads.**

Don't reuse virtual threads via a fixed pool — they're already cheap to create. Use `newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()` and let each task spawn its own. Pooling defeats the point.

**Mistake 3: Ignoring pinning.**

Heavy `synchronized` blocks with I/O inside will pin virtual threads to their carriers. Under load, throughput degrades. Detect with `-Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full`; fix by switching to `ReentrantLock`.
## Best practices
**1. For I/O-bound servers, use `newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()`.** One virtual thread per request; blocking code is now correct + fast.
**2. For CPU-bound work, keep platform threads.** Size to core count. Virtual threads add nothing for pure computation.
**3. Avoid `synchronized` in hot I/O paths; use `ReentrantLock`.** Prevents pinning.
**4. Adopt `StructuredTaskScope` (Java 21+ preview) for structured concurrency.** Scopes group related tasks so they can be cancelled and joined together — a huge readability win.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — 1 million sleeping virtual threads.** Confirm they complete in seconds using ~2 GB of memory. Try the same with platform threads and observe the crash.

**Why:** You'll never forget the scale difference.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Convert a thread-pool server to virtual threads.** Take a small HTTP server using `Executors.newFixedThreadPool(200)`. Swap for `newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()`. Measure throughput under load.

**Why:** This is the exact refactor real teams do now.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Detect pinning.** Deliberately write code that blocks inside a `synchronized` block. Run with `-Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full` and read the pin trace. Refactor to `ReentrantLock`.

**Why:** The one hazard you'll actually meet.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Structured concurrency.** Use `StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure` to run two API calls in parallel; cancel both if either fails.

**Why:** This is the future of async Java.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What are virtual threads, and what problem do they solve?**

**A:** Virtual threads (Java 21) are lightweight threads scheduled by the JVM — not the OS. Each is cheap (~few KB of memory) and millions can run concurrently. The problem they solve: **you cannot have a million OS threads** — each costs ~1 MB of stack, and the OS thread scheduler chokes long before that. Before Loom, high-concurrency servers had to use asynchronous / reactive code (WebFlux, RxJava) with callback chains — powerful but hard to read and debug. Virtual threads let you write simple, blocking, thread-per-request code — because when a virtual thread blocks on I/O, it unmounts from its carrier OS thread, freeing the carrier to run other virtual threads. Simple code + massive scale. It's Java's answer to Go's goroutines and Erlang's processes.

**Q2: How do virtual threads differ from platform threads?**

**A:** A **platform thread** is a thin wrapper over an OS thread — 1:1 with the kernel. Cost: ~1 MB stack, ~ms creation, thousands are the practical limit. **Virtual threads** are managed by the JVM. Many virtual threads are multiplexed onto a small pool of **carrier** platform threads. When a virtual thread blocks on I/O (`Thread.sleep`, blocking socket read, JDBC call), it *unmounts* from its carrier — freeing the carrier for other virtual threads. When the I/O completes, it *remounts* on any carrier. Cost: a few KB each; millions are practical. Behavior: same `Thread` API — you can `interrupt`, `join`, use `ThreadLocal`, etc. The magic is the unmount / remount step around blocking operations.

**Q3: What is pinning, and how do you avoid it?**

**A:** Pinning is when a virtual thread cannot unmount from its carrier during blocking — it holds the carrier hostage for the whole blocking duration. Two situations cause pinning: (1) blocking inside a `synchronized` block or method, and (2) calling native code (JNI) that blocks. Under heavy load with lots of pinning, all carriers get pinned and the whole virtual-thread pool starves. **Detection:** run with `-Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full` to log every pin event. **Fix:** replace `synchronized` with `ReentrantLock` in hot I/O paths (locks are pin-free). Java 24 aims to eliminate synchronized-caused pinning entirely, but until then, be aware. For most well-written code, pinning isn't an issue — it's mostly legacy libraries that misuse synchronized.

**Q4: Do virtual threads help CPU-bound workloads?**

**A:** No — virtual threads add no CPU. If you have 8 cores and 1000 virtual threads doing pure math, only 8 make progress at any moment. Virtual threads win only when threads spend most of their time **waiting** (I/O, sleeping, locks). For CPU-bound work, use a fixed platform-thread pool sized to `Runtime.availableProcessors()`. Rule: virtual threads for I/O concurrency; platform-thread pools for CPU-bound parallelism. In modern services, most requests are I/O-heavy — waiting on database, cache, downstream API — so virtual threads help enormously. But image-processing pipelines and matrix math still want their bounded platform pools.

**Q5: How do virtual threads change the thread-per-request server model?**

**A:** Traditional server model: fixed thread pool of ~200 threads handling all requests. When traffic spikes past 200 concurrent I/O-bound requests, latency degrades because requests queue for a thread even though the threads are just waiting on I/O. Solution before Loom: async / reactive code (WebFlux, RxJava, Netty) — powerful but complex, with callback chains, hard debugging, and lost stack traces. **With virtual threads:** just use `newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()`, one virtual thread per request. Blocking JDBC, blocking HTTP, blocking file I/O — all fine. The JVM multiplexes millions of virtual threads onto a few carriers. You write simple code that scales. This is why frameworks (Spring 6+, Helidon, Quarkus) are aggressively adopting virtual threads.

**Q6: What is structured concurrency?**

**A:** Structured concurrency (Java 21+ preview via `StructuredTaskScope`) treats concurrent tasks the way structured programming treats control flow — a scope opens, tasks run inside, and the scope guarantees they all complete (or are cancelled) before it closes. Classical async code lets a `Future` outlive the code that spawned it; a bug in one task can leak resources across the whole system. With structured concurrency: `try (var scope = new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure())` — inside, fork multiple subtasks. If one fails, the scope cancels the rest. When the try block ends, everything has either completed or been cancelled. This makes async code as easy to reason about as sequential code. Combined with virtual threads, this is the future of Java concurrency.

**Q7: What happens to my ThreadLocals when I switch to virtual threads?**

**A:** ThreadLocals still work — but they can become a memory problem because you might have millions of virtual threads, each with its own ThreadLocal copies. That's why Java 21 also introduced **scoped values** (`ScopedValue`) — immutable, well-bounded alternatives to ThreadLocal that don't carry per-thread copies indefinitely. Existing frameworks using ThreadLocals for context propagation (Spring's `TransactionSynchronizationManager`, MDC for logging) work, but the community is gradually migrating to scoped values for better memory behavior at virtual-thread scale.

## Real-world examples

- **Helidon 4, Spring Boot 3.2+, Quarkus** all offer virtual-thread executors for their web layers. One flag / property switches thread-per-request from platform to virtual — massive scale improvement.
- **JDBC connections stay the same** but you can now hold thousands of blocking connections concurrently without OS-thread exhaustion. Connection-pool sizing changes.
- **Async libraries (Reactor, RxJava) are quietly less attractive** — much of what they enabled is now possible with simpler blocking code.
- **Startup latency of high-concurrency servers dropped dramatically** — no need to warm up a big thread pool.
- **Go, Elixir, Kotlin coroutines** were the predecessors. Java's virtual threads bring the same benefit while keeping the whole Java ecosystem intact.

## Advanced notes

- **The carrier pool** by default uses `ForkJoinPool.commonPool()` sized to `availableProcessors()`. Tunable via system properties.
- **`ThreadLocal` on virtual threads** — inherited by default; can be enormously memory-hungry at scale. Java 21 introduced **`ScopedValue`** as the successor.
- **Continuations** — the JVM primitive underneath virtual threads. Not public API; you interact via `Thread`.
- **Cancellation** — `Thread.interrupt()` still works; virtual threads propagate `InterruptedException` on blocking calls.
- **Debugging** — thread dumps include virtual threads (with a special marker). JFR events capture mounts/unmounts.
- **Sun-shining synchronized** — JEP 491 (Java 24 preview) plans to eliminate synchronized-caused pinning by making the monitor mechanism virtual-thread-aware.
- **Reactive vs virtual** — for genuinely event-driven workflows (WebSockets, streaming pipelines), reactive frameworks remain valuable. For request/response servers, virtual threads have largely won.

flashcards.back: A lightweight thread scheduled by the JVM (not the OS). Millions can run concurrently. Unmounts from its carrier on blocking I/O.
Platform: ~1 MB stack, ~ms creation, thousands max. Virtual: few KB, ~µs creation, millions.
Virtual thread can't unmount because it's blocking inside a synchronized block or JNI. Fix: use ReentrantLock.

flashcards.front: Are virtual threads good for CPU-bound work?
flashcards.back: No — they don't add cores. Use bounded platform pools for CPU. Virtual threads are for I/O.

flashcards.front: One-liner to switch to virtual threads?

quiz.explain: JVM multiplexes millions of virtual threads onto a few carrier OS threads.
Unmount on blocking → thousands of concurrent I/O tasks per carrier.

options: A virtual thread blocks inside synchronized or JNI
quiz.explain: Only these two situations prevent unmount from a carrier.

quiz.q: For CPU-bound work you should use…
options: A fixed platform-thread pool sized to core count
quiz.explain: Virtual threads add no CPU; use bounded platform pool for CPU work.

quiz.q: The successor to ThreadLocal that fits virtual-thread scale is…
quiz.explain: ScopedValue (Java 21+) has better semantics for millions of virtual threads.

challenges.text: Spawn 1 million virtual threads that each sleep 1 second. Measure memory and elapsed time.
Print `Thread.currentThread().isVirtual()` inside a virtual-thread task and outside.
Convert an `Executors.newFixedThreadPool(200)` server to virtual threads. Measure throughput under load.
Compare virtual vs platform threads for a mocked 100ms I/O workload with 10,000 tasks. Which finishes first?
Deliberately cause pinning: block inside a `synchronized` block. Detect with `-Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full`. Fix with ReentrantLock.
Use StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure to run two API calls in parallel; cancel one if the other fails.
Benchmark: reactive (WebFlux/Reactor) vs virtual-thread blocking style under 10,000 concurrent requests. Report p50/p95/p99 latencies.
